q: A company has multiple AWS accounts and needs to enforce a policy that prevents any account from launching EC2 instances in unapproved Regions. Which solution achieves this?
o: Apply a Service Control Policy (SCP) in AWS Organizations that denies ec2:RunInstances for all Regions except the approved ones
Create IAM policies in each account that deny access to unapproved Regions, attached to every individual user
Configure security groups in each VPC to block traffic from unapproved Regions at the network level
Use Amazon GuardDuty to detect and automatically terminate instances launched in unapproved Regions
e: Correct (A): SCPs apply permission boundaries across all principals in member accounts. A Region-deny SCP prevents any identity in any account from launching resources in restricted Regions. Centrally managed. B is wrong: IAM policies per user are not scalable across multiple accounts and can be overridden by administrators. SCPs provide a hard boundary that even account admins cannot bypass. C is wrong: Security groups control network traffic (ports/protocols), not which Region resources are created in. They operate within a VPC, not across Regions. D is wrong: GuardDuty detects threats but doesn't enforce preventive controls. It's detective, not preventive. Instances would already be running before detection.

q: A solutions architect needs to allow an EC2 instance to access objects in an S3 bucket without storing long-term credentials on the instance. What is the MOST secure approach?
o: Create an IAM user with S3 access, generate access keys, and store them in environment variables on the EC2 instance
Attach an IAM role with an S3 access policy to the EC2 instance so it receives temporary credentials automatically via the instance metadata service
Make the S3 bucket public so any instance can access it without credentials
Store the S3 access keys in the application source code deployed on the instance
e: Correct (B): IAM roles attached to EC2 provide temporary credentials via the instance metadata service. Credentials rotate automatically. No long-term secrets stored anywhere. A is wrong: Access keys are long-term credentials that can be leaked, are hard to rotate, and violate the requirement of no long-term credentials on the instance. C is wrong: Public buckets expose data to the entire internet, not just the EC2 instance. This violates security principles entirely. D is wrong: Credentials in source code are the worst practice - they get committed to repos, shared with developers, and never rotated.

q: A company needs to encrypt all EBS volumes with customer-managed keys and ensure no volume can be created without encryption. What combination of controls achieves this?
o: Enable EBS encryption by default in the account settings using a customer-managed KMS key, and add an SCP denying ec2:CreateVolume unless aws:RequestedEncryption is true
Configure S3 bucket policies to require server-side encryption on all objects stored in the account
Use AWS WAF rules to intercept and block unencrypted volume creation API calls at the network edge
Enable CloudTrail logging and manually review volume creation events weekly for compliance
e: Correct (A): EBS encryption by default ensures new volumes use the specified CMK. The SCP adds a preventive guardrail that blocks any attempt to create unencrypted volumes even if the default setting is changed. Defense in depth. B is wrong: S3 bucket policies control S3 object encryption, not EBS volume encryption. These are different services with different encryption mechanisms. C is wrong: WAF filters HTTP/HTTPS web traffic, not AWS API calls for resource creation. It cannot intercept EC2 API calls. D is wrong: CloudTrail logging is detective (after the fact). Volumes would already be created unencrypted before anyone reviews the logs.

q: A web application on EC2 instances behind an ALB needs to accept HTTPS traffic from the internet. Where should the TLS certificate be terminated for LEAST operational overhead?
o: Terminate TLS at the Application Load Balancer using a certificate managed by AWS Certificate Manager, with HTTP to the backend instances
Install self-signed certificates on each EC2 instance and terminate TLS at the instance level with no load balancer involvement
Terminate TLS at the Route 53 DNS level before traffic reaches any AWS infrastructure
Use AWS WAF to decrypt HTTPS traffic and forward decrypted payloads to the ALB
e: Correct (A): ALB handles TLS termination natively. ACM provides free, auto-renewing certificates. No cert management on instances. Backend uses HTTP (within VPC, secure). Lowest operational overhead. B is wrong: Self-signed certs cause browser warnings, require manual renewal on every instance, don't scale with Auto Scaling, and increase operational burden significantly. C is wrong: Route 53 is a DNS service - it resolves names to IPs. It cannot terminate TLS connections or handle certificate management. D is wrong: WAF inspects HTTP traffic rules (SQLi, XSS) but doesn't decrypt TLS. WAF integrates with ALB/CloudFront which handle TLS termination.

q: A company needs to store database credentials that their application retrieves at runtime, with automatic rotation every 30 days. Which service?
o: AWS Secrets Manager which stores secrets, provides runtime retrieval via API, and supports automatic rotation with Lambda functions on configurable schedules
AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store SecureString which stores encrypted parameters but requires custom Lambda code for rotation scheduling
IAM user access keys which provide long-term credentials but do not support automatic rotation without custom tooling
Amazon S3 encrypted objects which can store credential files but require manual updates and don't integrate with RDS rotation
e: Correct (A): Secrets Manager: stores credentials, retrieves via API/SDK, native automatic rotation (built-in Lambda for RDS/Aurora/Redshift), configurable schedule (30/60/90 days). Purpose-built for this. B is wrong: Parameter Store SecureString stores encrypted values but has no built-in rotation mechanism. You must build and manage custom Lambda rotation yourself. C is wrong: IAM access keys are for AWS API access, not database credentials. They don't auto-rotate (max 2 keys, manual process). D is wrong: S3 stores files but has no rotation mechanism, no native RDS integration, and requires custom code for credential retrieval and updates.

q: A VPC has public and private subnets. Instances in the private subnet need to download patches from the internet but must NOT be directly reachable from the internet. Which component enables this?
o: A NAT Gateway in the public subnet that allows private subnet instances to initiate outbound connections while blocking inbound internet traffic
An Internet Gateway attached directly to the private subnet allowing bidirectional internet access
A bastion host that downloads all patches and manually copies them to each private instance via SCP
A VPC peering connection to another VPC that has internet access through its own gateway
e: Correct (A): NAT Gateway in public subnet: private instances route outbound traffic through it to the internet. Return traffic allowed (stateful). No inbound initiation from internet possible. One-way outbound. B is wrong: An IGW on a private subnet makes it public by definition. Instances get public IPs and are reachable from the internet, violating the requirement. C is wrong: A bastion host is for SSH access, not patch distribution. Manually copying patches doesn't scale and creates massive operational overhead. D is wrong: VPC peering connects two VPCs privately - it doesn't provide internet access. You'd still need a NAT Gateway or IGW somewhere.

q: A company uses AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO) to manage workforce access. They need developers to access a development account and a production account with different permission levels. What is the correct approach?
o: Create permission sets in IAM Identity Center with different policies for each account, and assign the developer group to each account with the appropriate permission set
Create separate IAM users in each account with different passwords and have developers log in separately to each account
Share a single root user credential across both accounts so developers can access everything from one login
Create a VPN connection between the accounts so developers in one account can automatically access the other
e: Correct (A): IAM Identity Center: create permission sets (e.g., DevFullAccess for dev, ReadOnly for prod), assign groups to accounts with appropriate sets. Single sign-on, centralized management. B is wrong: Separate IAM users per account means managing multiple credentials, no single sign-on, and operational overhead that Identity Center eliminates. C is wrong: Sharing root credentials is the most dangerous possible practice - no audit trail, no least privilege, no accountability, and root can't be restricted by SCPs. D is wrong: VPN connects networks, it doesn't provide identity management or differentiated permission levels between accounts.

q: An application stores sensitive customer data in S3. The company requires that data be encrypted at rest, encryption keys rotate annually, and they maintain full control over key deletion. Which KMS key type?
o: Customer managed key (CMK) which provides full control over key policy, rotation schedule configuration, and the ability to schedule key deletion with a waiting period
AWS managed key which AWS creates and rotates automatically but doesn't allow customer control over deletion or key policy customization
AWS owned key which AWS manages entirely with no visibility or control available to the customer
S3-managed key (SSE-S3) which encrypts with a key Amazon fully controls with no customer visibility into rotation or deletion
e: Correct (A): Customer managed keys: customer controls key policy (who can use/administer), enables/configures annual rotation, and can schedule deletion (7-30 day waiting period). Full control. B is wrong: AWS managed keys rotate every 3 years (recently changed to annual), but customers cannot modify key policy, cannot disable the key, and cannot schedule deletion. C is wrong: AWS owned keys are invisible to customers - no ARN, no CloudTrail logging, no control whatsoever. D is wrong: SSE-S3 keys are managed entirely by Amazon with no customer control over rotation, deletion, or access policies.

q: A company wants to centrally manage firewall rules across multiple accounts and automatically apply them to new resources. Which service?
o: AWS Firewall Manager which centrally configures and automatically applies WAF rules, Shield protections, security groups, and Network Firewall policies across Organization accounts
AWS WAF configured individually in each account by each team independently without central coordination
Security groups manually copied between accounts whenever a new VPC is created
Amazon Inspector scanning each account separately and generating reports that someone reviews manually
e: Correct (A): Firewall Manager: define security policies centrally, auto-apply to new accounts/resources. Manages WAF, Shield Advanced, SGs, Network Firewall, Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall across the org. B is wrong: Individual WAF configuration per account has no central governance, no consistency enforcement, and no automatic application to new resources. C is wrong: Manually copying security groups doesn't scale, has no automation, and diverges over time as teams make changes independently. D is wrong: Inspector scans for vulnerabilities (CVEs) - it doesn't manage or apply firewall rules across accounts.

q: A solutions architect needs to design a VPC architecture where the database tier is NEVER accessible from the internet, even if security groups are misconfigured. What provides this guarantee?
o: Place the database in a private subnet with no route to an Internet Gateway, ensuring no path exists for internet traffic regardless of security group configuration
Place the database in a public subnet but configure security groups to deny all inbound traffic from 0.0.0.0/0
Attach an Elastic IP to the database instance but configure the OS firewall to block all ports
Use Amazon CloudFront to hide the database endpoint behind a CDN distribution
e: Correct (A): Private subnets have no route to an IGW. Without a route, packets from the internet cannot reach the instance physically, regardless of security group rules. Network-level isolation. B is wrong: Public subnets have IGW routes. Security groups are the only defense - misconfiguration (the scenario stated) would expose the database immediately. C is wrong: Elastic IPs make instances internet-routable. OS firewalls can be misconfigured or disabled, providing no architectural guarantee. D is wrong: CloudFront serves web content - it doesn't hide databases. Databases don't serve HTTP content through CDN distributions.

q: A company needs to detect when IAM access keys haven't been rotated in 90 days and automatically notify the security team. Which combination of services?
o: AWS Config with a managed rule for access-key-rotation checking, triggering an EventBridge rule that publishes to an SNS topic notifying the security team
Amazon GuardDuty configured to scan for old access keys and send email alerts directly to administrators
AWS CloudTrail configured to block API calls from old access keys automatically after 90 days
IAM password policy settings that automatically delete access keys older than 90 days
e: Correct (A): Config managed rule (access-keys-rotated) evaluates key age. Non-compliant triggers EventBridge event. EventBridge routes to SNS. Security team receives notification. Fully automated detection. B is wrong: GuardDuty detects threats (compromised credentials, malicious activity), not credential hygiene like rotation compliance. No built-in key age checking. C is wrong: CloudTrail logs API calls but cannot block them. It's a recording service, not an enforcement mechanism. D is wrong: IAM password policies govern passwords (length, expiry), not access keys. There is no native IAM setting to auto-delete access keys.

q: A web application receives DDoS attacks targeting both the network layer (Layer 3/4) and the application layer (Layer 7). Which combination provides comprehensive protection?
o: AWS Shield Advanced for Layer 3/4 volumetric attacks combined with AWS WAF for Layer 7 application-layer attacks like HTTP floods and SQL injection
AWS Shield Standard alone which only provides basic Layer 3/4 protection with no application layer coverage or DRT support
Security groups configured to block all traffic which would also block legitimate users from accessing the application
Amazon CloudWatch alarms that detect high traffic and send notifications but take no automatic mitigation action
e: Correct (A): Shield Advanced: enhanced DDoS protection (L3/4), cost protection, DRT access. WAF: L7 rules (rate limiting, geo blocking, SQLi/XSS patterns). Together they cover all layers. B is wrong: Shield Standard provides basic L3/4 protection automatically (free) but no L7 protection, no DRT, no cost protection, and no advanced mitigation for sophisticated attacks. C is wrong: Blocking all traffic stops attacks but also stops legitimate users - it's a denial of service by your own configuration. D is wrong: CloudWatch detects but doesn't mitigate. Notifications inform humans but don't automatically block attack traffic.

q: A company needs their application to connect to an S3 bucket without traffic traversing the public internet. Which solution?
o: Create a VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3 that routes traffic through the AWS private network without crossing the internet
Configure a public NAT Gateway to route S3 traffic through the internet with TLS encryption for security
Create a CloudFront distribution in front of the S3 bucket and access it via the CDN URL
Open port 443 in the security group to allow HTTPS traffic to S3's public endpoint
e: Correct (A): VPC Gateway Endpoint for S3: traffic stays on AWS private network. No internet gateway, NAT, or public IP needed. Free. Add to route table. Restricts via endpoint policies. B is wrong: NAT Gateway routes through the internet (via IGW). Traffic leaves AWS's private network, which violates the requirement of not traversing the public internet. C is wrong: CloudFront caches S3 content at edge locations, but the origin fetch still occurs. This doesn't guarantee private network-only connectivity from VPC. D is wrong: Opening port 443 allows the connection but traffic still crosses the public internet to reach S3's public endpoint. The port isn't the issue - the network path is.

q: A company's compliance team requires that all data written to a specific S3 bucket is encrypted with a specific KMS key and no other key. How to enforce this?
o: Add an S3 bucket policy with a Deny statement that blocks s3:PutObject unless the request specifies the required KMS key ARN in the x-amz-server-side-encryption-aws-kms-key-id header
Enable S3 default encryption with the required key and trust that all applications will use the default without additional enforcement
Create an IAM policy allowing s3:PutObject only for administrators, preventing other users from uploading any objects
Use Amazon Macie to scan uploaded objects and delete any that weren't encrypted with the correct key after upload
e: Correct (A): Bucket policy Deny on PutObject unless condition matches exact KMS key ARN. Preventive control - upload is rejected immediately if wrong key (or no key) is specified. B is wrong: Default encryption applies when no encryption is specified in the request, but users can override it with a different key. No enforcement that the specific key must be used. C is wrong: Restricting uploads to admins doesn't solve the encryption key requirement - admins could still use the wrong key. D is wrong: Macie scans for sensitive data, not encryption compliance. Post-upload deletion means unencrypted data existed temporarily, violating compliance.

q: A solutions architect is designing cross-account access. Account A needs to allow a Lambda function in Account B to read from an S3 bucket in Account A. What is the MOST secure approach?
o: Create an IAM role in Account A with S3 read permissions and a trust policy allowing Account B's Lambda execution role to assume it via sts:AssumeRole
Generate long-term access keys in Account A and embed them in Account B's Lambda environment variables
Make the S3 bucket in Account A public so any principal including Account B's Lambda can read without credentials
Create an IAM user in Account A and share the username and password with Account B's development team
e: Correct (A): Cross-account role assumption: Account A creates role trusting Account B's Lambda role. Lambda assumes it, gets temporary credentials scoped to S3 read. No long-term secrets shared. B is wrong: Long-term access keys embedded in Lambda are insecure, hard to rotate, can be leaked, and violate temporary credential best practices. C is wrong: Public buckets expose data to the entire internet, not just Account B. Massive security risk for cross-account access. D is wrong: Sharing IAM user credentials between accounts violates security principles - no audit trail separation, long-term secrets, and no programmatic access for Lambda.

q: A company wants to prevent accidental deletion of critical S3 objects and ensure deleted objects can be recovered. Which TWO features should they enable?
o: S3 Versioning to retain all object versions and S3 Object Lock in Governance mode to prevent deletion without specific permissions
S3 Transfer Acceleration to speed up uploads and S3 Intelligent-Tiering to move objects between storage classes
S3 Cross-Region Replication to copy objects and CloudFront caching to serve objects from edge locations
S3 Lifecycle rules to transition objects to Glacier and S3 Requester Pays to charge downloaders
e: Correct (A): Versioning keeps every version (delete creates a delete marker, original version remains). Object Lock Governance mode prevents deletion unless user has s3:BypassGovernanceRetention. Recovery and protection. B is wrong: Transfer Acceleration speeds uploads, Intelligent-Tiering optimizes cost - neither prevents deletion or enables recovery. C is wrong: CRR copies objects to another Region (helps DR) but doesn't prevent deletion in either Region. CloudFront caches but doesn't protect source. D is wrong: Lifecycle transitions objects for cost (doesn't prevent deletion) and Requester Pays shifts download costs - neither provides deletion protection.

q: A solutions architect needs to log all API activity across all accounts in an AWS Organization and prevent any account from tampering with the logs. What is the recommended approach?
o: Create an Organization Trail in CloudTrail that logs to a centralized S3 bucket in a dedicated logging account with a bucket policy denying deletion from member accounts
Enable CloudTrail in each individual account logging to local S3 buckets managed by each account's administrators
Use VPC Flow Logs to capture all API calls across accounts since VPC Flow Logs record all AWS activity
Configure Amazon CloudWatch Logs in each account and trust each team to not modify their own log streams
e: Correct (A): Organization Trail: logs all accounts centrally. Dedicated logging account: member accounts can't access/delete. Bucket policy denies s3:DeleteObject from non-logging principals. Tamper-proof. B is wrong: Local logging per account means account admins can delete or modify their own CloudTrail logs, eliminating tamper protection. C is wrong: VPC Flow Logs capture network traffic metadata (IPs, ports, bytes), not API calls. They don't record who called which AWS API. D is wrong: CloudWatch Logs per account can be modified by account admins. No centralization, no tamper protection, no organizational governance.

q: A company runs a web application that must remain available even if an entire Availability Zone fails. The application uses an Auto Scaling group and an ALB. What is the MINIMUM architecture change to achieve this?
o: Configure the Auto Scaling group and ALB to span at least two Availability Zones so that if one AZ fails, instances in the other AZ continue serving traffic
Deploy all instances in a single AZ and rely on Auto Scaling to replace failed instances within the same AZ faster than the outage lasts
Create a second complete copy of the application in another AWS Region with active-active failover through Route 53
Deploy the application on a single larger instance type with enhanced networking to survive AZ failures through hardware resilience
e: Correct (A): Multi-AZ ASG + ALB: instances distributed across AZs. If one AZ fails, ALB routes to healthy instances in remaining AZs. Auto Scaling launches replacements. Minimum viable HA. B is wrong: Single AZ means complete outage when that AZ fails. Auto Scaling can't launch in a failed AZ. No resilience to AZ failure by definition. C is wrong: Multi-Region is far beyond minimum - it adds massive complexity (data sync, DNS failover, duplicate infrastructure). Multi-AZ within one Region suffices. D is wrong: A single instance in a failed AZ is unavailable regardless of its size. Hardware resilience doesn't protect against AZ-wide failures (power, networking).

q: A solutions architect is designing a microservices architecture where Service A produces messages that Service B processes. Service B occasionally goes offline for maintenance. What ensures no messages are lost?
o: Place an Amazon SQS queue between Service A and Service B so messages persist in the queue until Service B is available to process them
Have Service A call Service B directly via synchronous HTTP and retry indefinitely until Service B responds
Store messages in instance memory on Service A and wait for Service B to pull them when it comes back online
Configure Service A to simply drop messages when Service B is unavailable since the data is not critical
e: Correct (A): SQS decouples producers from consumers. Messages persist (up to 14 days retention) until processed. Service B processes the backlog when it returns from maintenance. Zero message loss. B is wrong: Synchronous retry blocks Service A indefinitely during B's maintenance. A causes cascading failures, B doesn't guarantee delivery if A crashes during retries. C is wrong: Instance memory is volatile - if Service A's instance restarts, reboots, or scales down, all messages in memory are permanently lost. D is wrong: The question states messages cannot be lost. Dropping messages violates the requirement regardless of perceived criticality.

q: A company needs a disaster recovery strategy with an RTO of 1-2 hours and an RPO of minutes. They have budget constraints. Which DR strategy fits?
o: Warm standby which maintains a scaled-down but functional copy of the production environment in another Region, ready to scale up within 1-2 hours
Multi-site active-active which runs full production in two Regions simultaneously with real-time synchronization, exceeding budget constraints
Backup and restore which stores backups in another Region but requires hours to provision infrastructure and restore data, exceeding RTO
Pilot light which keeps only core database replicated but requires significant time to rebuild compute and application tiers, potentially exceeding RTO
e: Correct (A): Warm standby: smaller but running environment (DB replicas, minimal compute). Scale up during failover. RTO 1-2 hours achievable. RPO minutes via continuous replication. Cost-moderate. B is wrong: Active-active meets RTO/RPO easily but costs 2x (full duplicate production). The budget constraint eliminates this option. C is wrong: Backup and restore has RTO of hours to days (provision infrastructure, restore from snapshots). Can't reliably meet 1-2 hour RTO for complex applications. D is wrong: Pilot light keeps only data layer running. Rebuilding entire compute/app tier from scratch can exceed 1-2 hours for complex architectures.

q: A company wants to migrate a monolithic application to microservices on AWS. The application currently handles user authentication, order processing, and inventory management in a single codebase. Which approach enables independent scaling?
o: Decompose into separate services (auth, orders, inventory) running in containers on ECS with an ALB routing to each service, communicating via SQS for async operations
Keep the monolith on a single large EC2 instance and scale vertically by upgrading to a bigger instance type when load increases
Deploy three copies of the entire monolith and route different URL paths to different copies even though each copy contains all code
Rewrite everything as a single Lambda function that handles all three domains in one execution context
e: Correct (A): Separate containers per service: each scales independently based on its own demand. ALB path-based routing directs traffic. SQS decouples async work. True microservices. B is wrong: Vertical scaling has hard limits (largest instance type) and requires downtime to resize. The monolith can't scale one component independently. C is wrong: Three copies of the full monolith waste resources - each copy runs unused code. Not microservices - just three monoliths with routing. D is wrong: A single Lambda with all logic is still a monolith (single deployment unit). Can't scale auth independently from orders. Lambda has size/timeout limits.

q: An application uses Amazon RDS MySQL with a Multi-AZ deployment. During a failover event, what happens to the database endpoint?
o: The DNS CNAME record automatically points to the standby instance which is promoted to primary, with no application connection string changes required
The application must be manually updated with the new IP address of the standby instance after failover completes
Both the primary and standby instances become read-only until an administrator manually promotes one of them
A completely new database instance is provisioned from the latest backup, which takes 30-60 minutes
e: Correct (A): Multi-AZ failover: RDS flips the DNS CNAME to the standby (now primary). Application reconnects to same endpoint. Automatic. Typically 60-120 seconds. No code changes. B is wrong: The endpoint DNS record updates automatically - applications using the RDS endpoint never need manual IP changes. That's the point of Multi-AZ. C is wrong: The standby is automatically promoted to read-write primary. No manual intervention. No read-only state for the new primary. D is wrong: Multi-AZ uses synchronous replication to a standby - failover promotes the existing standby. No new instance provisioning or backup restoration needed.

q: A solutions architect needs to design a system that processes uploaded images asynchronously. Uploads are sporadic - sometimes 1,000 per hour, sometimes zero. Which architecture minimizes cost while handling all uploads?
o: S3 event notification triggers a Lambda function for each upload that processes the image, scaling to zero when no uploads occur
A fleet of EC2 instances running 24/7 polling S3 for new uploads every second with Auto Scaling set to minimum 3 instances
An ECS cluster with 10 always-running tasks that process a shared SQS queue of upload notifications
A single large EC2 instance with a cron job that checks S3 every 5 minutes for new files to process
e: Correct (A): S3 event → Lambda: zero cost when idle (no uploads). Scales automatically to concurrent executions when 1,000 uploads arrive. Pay only per invocation + duration. Serverless. B is wrong: Minimum 3 instances running 24/7 costs money during zero-upload periods. Massive waste for sporadic workloads. C is wrong: 10 always-running ECS tasks cost money continuously regardless of whether any images need processing. Over-provisioned for sporadic load. D is wrong: Single instance running 24/7 has fixed cost during idle periods, 5-minute polling delay, and can't handle 1,000 uploads/hour if processing is slow.

q: A company needs to route traffic to a healthy secondary Region if their primary Region becomes unavailable. Which Route 53 routing policy achieves automatic failover?
o: Failover routing policy with health checks that automatically routes traffic to the secondary Region when the primary Region's health check fails
Simple routing policy which distributes traffic randomly without considering endpoint health status
Weighted routing policy which splits traffic by percentage but continues sending traffic to unhealthy endpoints
Geolocation routing policy which routes based on user geography but doesn't consider endpoint availability
e: Correct (A): Failover routing: primary record + secondary record. Health check monitors primary. When primary fails health check, DNS resolves to secondary automatically. B is wrong: Simple routing returns all values (no health-check-based failover). Doesn't distinguish between primary and secondary or check health. C is wrong: Weighted routing distributes by weight proportions. Without failover configuration, it continues routing to unhealthy endpoints (unless health checks explicitly remove them). D is wrong: Geolocation routes by user location, not by endpoint health. Users in a specific geography always go to the same endpoint regardless of availability.

q: A stateless web application runs on EC2 behind an ALB. Users report session loss when instances are terminated during scale-in. How to fix this while maintaining elasticity?
o: Externalize session state to Amazon ElastiCache Redis so sessions persist independently of any specific EC2 instance lifecycle
Enable sticky sessions on the ALB to pin users to specific instances, which reintroduces the problem if that instance is terminated
Store session data in local instance memory and increase the scale-in cooldown period to 24 hours hoping users finish
Disable Auto Scaling entirely so instances are never terminated and sessions are never lost
e: Correct (A): External session store (ElastiCache Redis): sessions survive instance termination. Any instance serves any user. True stateless compute. Elastic scaling works perfectly. B is wrong: Sticky sessions pin users to instances, but the problem IS instance termination during scale-in. Pinned users lose sessions when their instance terminates. Doesn't solve the root cause. C is wrong: Local memory sessions are lost when instances terminate regardless of cooldown period. Eventually scale-in happens and sessions are lost. D is wrong: Disabling Auto Scaling eliminates elasticity entirely. You pay for peak capacity 24/7 and lose the cost/performance benefits of scaling.

q: A company's application must have a Recovery Time Objective (RTO) of less than 10 minutes for their database tier. Which RDS configuration meets this?
o: RDS Multi-AZ deployment which provides automatic failover to a synchronous standby replica in 60-120 seconds with no data loss
RDS Single-AZ with automated backups that restore from the latest snapshot which takes 30-60 minutes depending on database size
RDS read replicas in the same AZ which serve read traffic but require manual promotion taking 5-10 minutes plus DNS propagation
RDS with manual snapshots taken weekly which requires restoring to a new instance from the snapshot
e: Correct (A): Multi-AZ failover: automatic, 60-120 seconds, DNS CNAME flip, zero data loss (synchronous replication). Well within 10-minute RTO. B is wrong: Restoring from snapshot provisions a new instance and replays transaction logs - typically 30-60+ minutes for large databases. Exceeds 10-minute RTO. C is wrong: Read replica promotion requires manual intervention, takes 5-10 minutes, plus application reconnection. Borderline and not automatic - risky for a firm 10-minute RTO. D is wrong: Weekly manual snapshots mean up to 7 days of data loss (RPO issue) and restoration takes 30+ minutes (RTO issue). Fails both metrics.

q: A solutions architect is designing a multi-tier architecture. The web tier must scale independently from the application tier. What decoupling mechanism between tiers provides the MOST reliability?
o: An SQS queue between the web tier and application tier that buffers requests, allowing each tier to scale independently based on its own metrics
Direct synchronous HTTP calls from web tier to application tier where the web tier blocks until the application tier responds
A shared NFS file system where the web tier writes request files and the application tier polls for new files every second
A shared RDS database table where the web tier inserts rows and the application tier queries for unprocessed rows
e: Correct (A): SQS decouples completely. Web tier sends to queue and responds to user immediately. App tier processes at its own pace. Each scales on its own metrics (web: request count, app: queue depth). B is wrong: Synchronous coupling means web tier performance depends on app tier. If app tier is slow or down, web tier blocks and users see errors. Not independent scaling. C is wrong: NFS file polling adds latency (1-second intervals), creates I/O bottlenecks, and the file system becomes a single point of failure. Not designed for message passing. D is wrong: Database as a message queue creates polling overhead, row locking contention, scaling limitations, and the database becomes a bottleneck. Anti-pattern.

q: A company uses AWS Step Functions to orchestrate a multi-step order processing workflow. One step calls an external payment API that occasionally times out. How should the architect handle this?
o: Configure the Step Functions state with retry logic using exponential backoff and a catch block that routes to a failure handling state after max retries
Allow the entire workflow to fail and rely on a human operator to manually resubmit failed orders during business hours
Set an infinite timeout on the payment API call so it never times out regardless of how long the external service takes to respond
Skip the payment step entirely when it fails and ship the order without payment confirmation
e: Correct (A): Step Functions native Retry (exponential backoff, max attempts) handles transient failures automatically. Catch routes to error handling (notify customer, queue for retry). Resilient pattern. B is wrong: Manual human intervention doesn't scale, introduces delays, and fails outside business hours. Order processing needs automated resilience. C is wrong: Infinite timeout blocks the workflow indefinitely if the API never responds. Resources consumed, customer waiting, no resolution. D is wrong: Shipping without payment confirmation causes revenue loss and fraud exposure. Business logic requires payment before fulfillment.

q: A company needs their S3 data to be recoverable even if the entire primary Region becomes unavailable. Which feature provides this?
o: S3 Cross-Region Replication which asynchronously copies objects to a bucket in a different Region providing geographic redundancy
S3 versioning which keeps multiple versions of objects but only within the same Region and same bucket
S3 Intelligent-Tiering which moves objects between access tiers for cost optimization within the same Region
S3 Transfer Acceleration which speeds up uploads using CloudFront edge locations but doesn't replicate data
e: Correct (A): Cross-Region Replication: asynchronous copy to bucket in another Region. If primary Region is unavailable, data exists independently in the destination Region. Geographic DR. B is wrong: Versioning protects against accidental deletion/overwrite within a single Region. If the entire Region is unavailable, versioned objects in that Region are also unavailable. C is wrong: Intelligent-Tiering optimizes cost by moving between access tiers. All copies remain in the same Region - no geographic redundancy. D is wrong: Transfer Acceleration speeds data uploads through edge locations but doesn't create copies in other Regions. No redundancy benefit.

q: An application uses DynamoDB as its database. During peak events, the application experiences throttling. Which solution provides the MOST cost-effective way to handle unpredictable traffic spikes?
o: Switch to DynamoDB on-demand capacity mode which automatically scales to handle any traffic level and charges per request with no capacity planning
Provision maximum possible read/write capacity units permanently to handle peak load even during off-peak hours
Add an ElastiCache cluster in front of DynamoDB and cache all reads, which doesn't help if throttling occurs on writes
Migrate to Amazon RDS which handles traffic spikes better than DynamoDB through connection pooling
e: Correct (A): On-demand mode: instant scaling to any traffic level. Pay-per-request. No throttling from under-provisioning. Ideal for unpredictable/spiky workloads. Cost-effective because you only pay for actual requests. B is wrong: Provisioning for peak means paying for maximum capacity 24/7 including off-peak. Extremely wasteful for unpredictable spikes that occur briefly. C is wrong: Caching helps reads but the question mentions throttling generally. Write-heavy spikes aren't solved by read caching. Also adds infrastructure cost/complexity. D is wrong: RDS doesn't inherently handle spikes better - it requires instance sizing decisions and can hit connection limits. DynamoDB on-demand is purpose-built for this.

q: A solutions architect needs to distribute incoming API requests across three microservices based on the URL path (/users, /orders, /inventory). Which load balancer type and feature?
o: Application Load Balancer with path-based routing rules that direct requests to different target groups based on the URL path
Network Load Balancer which operates at Layer 4 (TCP) and cannot inspect URL paths for routing decisions
Classic Load Balancer which provides basic round-robin distribution without path-based routing capability
Gateway Load Balancer which routes traffic to virtual appliances for inspection not application backends
e: Correct (A): ALB operates at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS). Path-based rules: /users → user service target group, /orders → order service TG, /inventory → inventory TG. Purpose-built for microservices. B is wrong: NLB operates at Layer 4 (TCP/UDP). It sees IP addresses and ports, not HTTP paths. Cannot route based on URL path content. C is wrong: Classic LB provides basic routing (round-robin/connection-based) without URL path inspection. It's also deprecated for new deployments. D is wrong: Gateway LB routes to security/network virtual appliances (firewalls, IDS) for transparent inspection, not application microservice backends.

q: A company needs to ensure their application automatically replaces unhealthy EC2 instances without manual intervention. Which combination of services provides this?
o: Auto Scaling group health checks combined with ALB target group health checks that mark unhealthy instances for automatic replacement
CloudWatch alarms that send email notifications to administrators who then manually terminate and replace failed instances
AWS Config rules that detect non-compliant instances but do not take automated remediation actions
Route 53 health checks that update DNS records but do not terminate or replace failed instances
e: Correct (A): ASG uses health checks (EC2 status checks + ALB health checks). Unhealthy instance → ASG terminates it → launches replacement automatically. Self-healing. Zero manual intervention. B is wrong: Email notifications require human action - not automated. People miss emails, are in meetings, or asleep. Doesn't meet 'without manual intervention' requirement. C is wrong: Config detects non-compliance but doesn't automatically terminate/replace instances. It's detective, not auto-remediation (unless you build custom Lambda). D is wrong: Route 53 health checks remove unhealthy endpoints from DNS but don't terminate instances or launch replacements. The broken instance keeps running.

q: A company processes orders through a workflow: validate → charge payment → update inventory → send confirmation. If the payment step fails, the entire transaction must roll back. Which pattern on AWS?
o: Saga pattern implemented with AWS Step Functions where each step has a compensating action that undoes the previous step's work if a later step fails
Two-phase commit across all services simultaneously which is not natively supported in distributed microservices architectures
Process all steps synchronously in a single Lambda function with a 15-minute timeout hoping it completes before timing out
Store all steps in an SQS FIFO queue and process them one at a time with no rollback mechanism if a step fails
e: Correct (A): Saga pattern: each step has a compensation (undo). If payment fails: compensate validation. Step Functions orchestrates the sequence and routes to compensation steps on failure. Distributed transaction. B is wrong: Two-phase commit requires tight coupling and distributed locks across services. Impractical in microservices. No AWS-native support for cross-service 2PC. C is wrong: Single Lambda with all logic is a monolith. 15-minute timeout risks. No graceful rollback if it times out mid-transaction. Not resilient. D is wrong: FIFO queues ensure ordering but provide no rollback mechanism. If payment fails, inventory update was already processed with no way to compensate.

q: A company stores 50 TB of log data in S3 and needs to run ad-hoc SQL queries against it without provisioning any infrastructure. Which service?
o: Amazon Athena which provides serverless interactive SQL queries directly against S3 data in formats like Parquet, JSON, and CSV with pay-per-query pricing
Amazon Redshift which requires provisioning a cluster of nodes before running queries against imported data
Amazon RDS which requires loading data into a relational database engine before SQL queries can execute
AWS Glue which performs ETL transformations but does not provide interactive SQL query capabilities directly
e: Correct (A): Athena: serverless, queries S3 directly using standard SQL. No infrastructure to manage. Pay per TB scanned. Supports Parquet, ORC, JSON, CSV. Ideal for ad-hoc analysis. B is wrong: Redshift requires provisioning a cluster (nodes, storage), loading data, and ongoing management. Not serverless, not zero-infrastructure. C is wrong: RDS requires provisioning an instance, creating a schema, and loading 50 TB of data before querying. Significant effort and cost. D is wrong: Glue discovers schemas and transforms data (ETL) but doesn't provide the interactive query interface. Athena uses Glue Data Catalog but runs queries itself.

q: A company needs a storage solution for an HPC (high-performance computing) workload that requires sub-millisecond latency and high throughput shared across hundreds of compute instances. Which service?
o: Amazon FSx for Lustre which provides a high-performance parallel file system designed for HPC with sub-millisecond latency and hundreds of GB/s throughput
Amazon EFS which provides shared NFS storage but with higher latency than Lustre and lower maximum throughput for HPC workloads
Amazon S3 which provides object storage with high durability but higher latency than file systems and no POSIX interface
Amazon EBS which provides block storage attached to a single instance and cannot be shared across hundreds of instances simultaneously
e: Correct (A): FSx for Lustre: parallel file system built for HPC. Sub-ms latency, hundreds of GB/s throughput, POSIX-compliant, integrates with S3. Purpose-built for compute-intensive workloads. B is wrong: EFS provides shared NFS but optimizes for general-purpose workloads. Lower max throughput than Lustre and higher latency for HPC-scale I/O patterns. C is wrong: S3 is object storage accessed via API (PUT/GET). No POSIX file system interface, higher latency per operation, not suitable for HPC random I/O. D is wrong: EBS volumes attach to a single instance (except Multi-Attach for io2 which supports max 16 instances). Cannot share across hundreds of instances.

q: A solutions architect needs to choose a database for an application that performs millions of key-value lookups per second with single-digit millisecond latency. Data model is simple: user_id → profile JSON. Which database?
o: Amazon DynamoDB which provides single-digit millisecond latency for key-value access at any scale with automatic partitioning and no schema management
Amazon RDS PostgreSQL which provides relational capabilities with SQL joins but higher latency for simple key-value lookups at millions of requests per second
Amazon Redshift which is optimized for analytical queries across large datasets not high-throughput transactional key-value access
Amazon Neptune which is a graph database designed for relationship traversal queries not simple key-value lookups
e: Correct (A): DynamoDB: purpose-built for key-value. Partition key (user_id) → item (profile JSON). Single-digit ms at any scale. Auto-partitions. No connection limits. Millions of RPS with on-demand. B is wrong: RDS PostgreSQL handles key-value via primary key lookup but connection pooling limits throughput. At millions of RPS, connection management becomes a bottleneck. Higher per-request cost. C is wrong: Redshift is OLAP (columnar, analytical queries). Optimized for complex aggregations across TB/PB, not millions of individual key-value lookups per second. D is wrong: Neptune stores graph relationships (nodes, edges, traversals). Overkill and wrong access pattern for simple user_id → JSON document lookups.

q: A company's application reads the same database rows millions of times per hour but the data only changes every 5 minutes. Database CPU is at 90%. How to reduce database load while maintaining low read latency?
o: Add an Amazon ElastiCache Redis cluster in front of the database with a 5-minute TTL so repeated reads are served from sub-millisecond in-memory cache
Add more RDS read replicas until database CPU drops below 50% which adds cost and management complexity
Increase the RDS instance size to the largest available which is expensive and has a hardware ceiling
Move the application to larger EC2 instances hoping fewer database connections reduce database CPU
e: Correct (A): ElastiCache Redis: cache frequently read data in memory. 5-min TTL matches data change frequency. Millions of reads hit cache (sub-ms) instead of database. DB CPU drops dramatically. B is wrong: Read replicas distribute load but each replica is a full database instance. Expensive scaling for a problem that caching solves more cheaply. Also adds replication lag considerations. C is wrong: Vertical scaling (bigger instance) has a ceiling (largest instance type). Expensive. Doesn't solve the fundamental problem of redundant reads hitting the database. D is wrong: Larger EC2 instances don't reduce database queries. The application still makes the same millions of reads. Database CPU stays at 90% regardless of app instance size.

q: A company needs to ingest and process millions of streaming events per second in real-time from IoT sensors. Which service provides this capability?
o: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams which ingests millions of records per second with configurable shard capacity for real-time stream processing
Amazon SQS which provides message queuing but is designed for asynchronous decoupling not real-time streaming analytics at millions of events per second
AWS Batch which processes batch computing jobs on a schedule but is not designed for continuous real-time streaming
Amazon S3 which stores objects durably but does not provide real-time stream ingestion or processing capabilities
e: Correct (A): Kinesis Data Streams: ingest millions of records/second. Shards scale horizontally. Consumers process in real-time (sub-second). Replay capability. Purpose-built for streaming. B is wrong: SQS is pull-based message queuing for decoupling services. Not designed for ordered, replayable, real-time stream processing at IoT scale. No time-window analytics. C is wrong: Batch runs scheduled compute jobs (start, process, finish). Not continuous real-time ingestion. Designed for finite batch workloads, not infinite streams. D is wrong: S3 stores objects (PUT then GET). No real-time ingestion of millions of events/second with sub-second processing. High latency for this pattern.

q: A solutions architect needs to serve a global user base with low latency for static assets (images, CSS, JavaScript). Origin is an S3 bucket in us-east-1. Which architecture?
o: Amazon CloudFront distribution with the S3 bucket as origin, caching content at 400+ edge locations worldwide so users download from the nearest edge
Replicate the S3 bucket to every Region manually and use Route 53 latency-based routing to direct users to the nearest bucket
Deploy EC2 web servers in every Region serving static files from local EBS volumes with manual synchronization
Use S3 Transfer Acceleration to speed up downloads which optimizes the upload path not the download delivery to end users
e: Correct (A): CloudFront caches static content at edge locations globally. First request fetches from origin (S3). Subsequent requests served from edge (sub-10ms for cached content). No infrastructure management. B is wrong: Manual replication to every Region is expensive (storage × Regions), operationally complex (sync logic), and doesn't provide edge-level caching within each Region. C is wrong: EC2 servers per Region adds massive operational overhead (patching, scaling, sync). Far more complex than CDN for static content serving. D is wrong: Transfer Acceleration optimizes uploads TO S3 (using edge locations as ingestion points). It doesn't cache or accelerate downloads FROM S3 to end users.

q: A company needs to choose between Amazon Aurora and Amazon RDS MySQL for a write-heavy transactional workload with 99.99% availability requirement. Which is the better choice and why?
o: Amazon Aurora because it provides 6-way replication across 3 AZs, self-healing storage, and up to 99.99% availability with Multi-AZ configuration, outperforming standard RDS for write-heavy workloads
Amazon RDS MySQL because it is always cheaper and performs identically to Aurora for all workload types with the same availability guarantees
Neither - use DynamoDB instead because relational databases cannot handle write-heavy workloads at any scale
Amazon RDS MySQL with a single-AZ deployment because Multi-AZ adds write latency from synchronous replication
e: Correct (A): Aurora: distributed storage (6 copies, 3 AZs), tolerates 2 copy failures for reads, 1 for writes. Self-healing. 5x MySQL write throughput. Multi-AZ Aurora provides 99.99% SLA. B is wrong: RDS MySQL provides 99.95% SLA (Multi-AZ). Aurora provides 99.99%. Aurora's distributed storage architecture provides better write performance and higher availability. Not identical. C is wrong: Relational databases handle write-heavy workloads fine - Aurora specifically optimizes for it with parallel write I/O across distributed storage. DynamoDB is for different access patterns. D is wrong: Single-AZ provides no HA (99.99% impossible). Multi-AZ does add minor write latency but Aurora's architecture minimizes this with quorum writes.

q: A company needs to transform raw CSV files in S3 into Parquet format for optimized Athena queries. Which serverless option handles this transformation?
o: AWS Glue ETL job with a PySpark script that reads CSV from S3, transforms to Parquet, and writes back to S3 without provisioning any infrastructure
A fleet of EC2 instances running Apache Spark that you manage, patch, and scale manually based on data volume
Amazon RDS stored procedures that read CSV files and output Parquet format which is not a capability RDS provides
Amazon CloudFront which caches and serves files but cannot transform data formats between CSV and Parquet
e: Correct (A): Glue: serverless Spark. Define source (S3 CSV), transformation (schema mapping), sink (S3 Parquet). Glue provisions workers, runs job, terminates. No infrastructure management. B is wrong: Self-managed Spark on EC2 works but requires provisioning, managing, patching, and scaling clusters manually. Not serverless. Significant operational overhead. C is wrong: RDS is a relational database. It cannot read arbitrary S3 files or output Parquet format. Wrong tool entirely. D is wrong: CloudFront is a CDN that caches and serves content. It has zero data transformation capability - it serves files as-is.

q: A solutions architect needs to choose a load balancer for an application that requires static IP addresses for firewall whitelisting by partner companies. Which load balancer?
o: Network Load Balancer which provides static IP addresses per AZ (one Elastic IP per AZ) while handling millions of requests per second at Layer 4
Application Load Balancer which provides dynamic IP addresses that change over time making firewall whitelisting by IP unreliable
Classic Load Balancer which provides dynamic DNS names without static IPs and is deprecated for new deployments
Gateway Load Balancer which routes to virtual appliances and does not provide customer-facing static IP addresses
e: Correct (A): NLB provides one static IP per AZ (can assign Elastic IPs). Partners whitelist these fixed IPs in their firewalls. Ultra-low latency, millions of RPS at Layer 4. B is wrong: ALB IP addresses change as AWS scales the load balancer. Partners cannot reliably whitelist dynamic IPs. ALB provides a DNS name, not static IPs. C is wrong: Classic LB also provides dynamic IPs behind a DNS name. No static IP support. Also deprecated - not recommended for new architectures. D is wrong: GWLB routes to security appliances (transparent inspection). Not designed as a customer-facing load balancer with static IPs.

q: A company wants to reduce Athena query costs and improve performance on their 10 TB dataset. The most common queries filter by date and customer_id. What optimization?
o: Partition the S3 data by date and customer_id and convert to Parquet columnar format so Athena scans only relevant partitions and columns
Store all data in a single large CSV file without partitions so Athena can read it in one sequential scan
Increase the Athena query timeout to allow longer scans of the full dataset without optimization
Move all data to Amazon RDS and run queries there instead since relational databases are always faster than Athena
e: Correct (A): Partitioning by date/customer_id means Athena reads only matching S3 prefixes (skips irrelevant data). Parquet is columnar (reads only needed columns). Both reduce TB scanned = lower cost + faster. B is wrong: Single large CSV means Athena scans the entire 10 TB for every query. Maximum cost (pay per TB scanned) and slowest possible performance. C is wrong: Longer timeout doesn't reduce cost or improve performance. You're still scanning unnecessary data and paying for every byte. D is wrong: Loading 10 TB into RDS is impractical (storage limits), expensive (large instance), and RDS isn't designed for ad-hoc analytics across TB-scale data.

q: A company needs to process video transcoding jobs. Jobs arrive in bursts (100 files at once, then nothing for hours). Each job takes 10-20 minutes. Which compute solution is MOST cost-effective?
o: AWS Batch with Spot Instances that provisions compute only when jobs arrive, processes the burst, and terminates instances when the queue is empty
A fixed fleet of 20 EC2 On-Demand instances running 24/7 waiting for transcoding jobs to arrive at any time
AWS Lambda functions configured to process each video which exceeds Lambda's 15-minute timeout and 10 GB memory limit for large videos
A single EC2 instance processing jobs sequentially which takes 20+ hours to clear a burst of 100 files
e: Correct (A): Batch: submit jobs to queue, Batch provisions Spot compute when jobs arrive, processes in parallel, terminates when done. Spot = up to 90% savings. Zero cost between bursts. B is wrong: 20 On-Demand instances running 24/7 for bursty workloads (hours of idle) wastes enormous money on idle compute between bursts. C is wrong: Lambda has 15-minute timeout - 10-20 minute video transcoding exceeds this. Also memory/storage limits for large video processing. D is wrong: Sequential processing on one instance means 100 × 15 min = 25 hours to clear a burst. Unacceptable latency for time-sensitive content.

q: A solutions architect is designing a data lake on S3. They need a centralized metadata catalog that tracks schemas, partitions, and table definitions for use by Athena, Redshift Spectrum, and EMR. Which service?
o: AWS Glue Data Catalog which provides a centralized Apache Hive-compatible metastore shared across Athena, Redshift Spectrum, EMR, and other analytics services
Amazon RDS with a custom schema table that each service queries before accessing S3 data directly
A JSON file stored in S3 that lists all table schemas which each service reads and parses independently
AWS CloudFormation templates that define data schemas as infrastructure-as-code resources
e: Correct (A): Glue Data Catalog: centralized metastore (databases, tables, partitions, schemas). Hive-compatible. Athena, Redshift Spectrum, EMR, and Glue jobs all reference it. Single source of truth. B is wrong: Custom RDS metadata store requires building and maintaining integration with each analytics service. Not natively integrated. Operational overhead. C is wrong: JSON file approach requires each service to implement parsing logic. No schema evolution, no partition management, no integration with AWS analytics services. D is wrong: CloudFormation defines infrastructure resources, not data schemas. It can't serve as a runtime metadata catalog queried by analytics engines.

q: A company needs to migrate their on-premises Oracle database to Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL. The schemas are incompatible between Oracle and PostgreSQL. Which AWS tool helps convert the schema?
o: AWS Schema Conversion Tool (SCT) which analyzes source database schemas and converts them to the target engine format, flagging items requiring manual intervention
AWS Database Migration Service which replicates data between databases but does not convert schema syntax or stored procedures between different engines
Amazon RDS which hosts databases but does not provide schema conversion tools for heterogeneous migrations
AWS CloudFormation which provisions infrastructure but cannot analyze or convert database schemas
e: Correct (A): SCT: analyzes Oracle schemas (tables, views, stored procedures, functions), converts to PostgreSQL-compatible syntax. Flags incompatible items needing manual rewrite. Essential for heterogeneous migrations. B is wrong: DMS replicates data (full load + CDC) between databases but doesn't convert schema objects. It assumes compatible schema already exists at target. SCT handles schema, DMS handles data. C is wrong: RDS provides the hosting platform (Aurora PostgreSQL) but no tools to convert from Oracle syntax to PostgreSQL syntax. D is wrong: CloudFormation provisions AWS resources. It has zero capability for analyzing database schemas or converting between database engine formats.

q: An application needs to make 100,000 read requests per second to DynamoDB with the lowest possible latency. Standard DynamoDB provides single-digit millisecond latency. How to achieve microsecond latency?
o: Add DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) which provides an in-memory cache in front of DynamoDB delivering microsecond response times for read-heavy workloads
Increase DynamoDB provisioned read capacity units to 1 million which reduces latency from single-digit milliseconds to microseconds
Create 100 DynamoDB read replicas across Regions to distribute the 100K RPS evenly and reduce per-item latency
Switch to Amazon Redshift which provides faster reads than DynamoDB for high-throughput transactional workloads
e: Correct (A): DAX: in-memory cache for DynamoDB. Microsecond latency for cached reads. API-compatible (drop-in replacement). Handles millions of requests. Purpose-built for this exact scenario. B is wrong: More RCUs prevent throttling but don't reduce latency below DynamoDB's architecture limit (single-digit ms). More capacity ≠ lower latency. C is wrong: DynamoDB doesn't have 'read replicas' like RDS. Global Tables replicate across Regions but add latency (cross-Region). They solve availability, not latency reduction. D is wrong: Redshift is an analytical data warehouse (OLAP). It's slower than DynamoDB for individual key-value lookups and not designed for 100K point reads/second.

q: A solutions architect needs to connect a VPC to hundreds of other VPCs and on-premises networks. VPC peering would create an unmanageable mesh. Which service simplifies this?
o: AWS Transit Gateway which acts as a central hub connecting hundreds of VPCs and on-premises networks through a single gateway with centralized routing
VPC peering between every pair of VPCs creating n*(n-1)/2 connections which becomes unmanageable at scale as stated in the question
A single large VPC containing all resources in one network with no segmentation which violates security and isolation principles
AWS Direct Connect to each VPC individually which requires hundreds of separate physical connections
e: Correct (A): Transit Gateway: hub-and-spoke. All VPCs and VPNs connect to one gateway. Centralized route tables. Scales to thousands of connections. Eliminates peering mesh complexity. B is wrong: The question explicitly states VPC peering creates unmanageable mesh. With 100 VPCs, that's 4,950 peering connections. Not scalable. C is wrong: Single VPC eliminates network isolation between workloads. No security boundaries, blast radius of issues affects everything, no multi-account governance. D is wrong: Direct Connect connects on-premises to AWS, not VPC-to-VPC. One Direct Connect with Transit Gateway VIF connects to all VPCs through the gateway.

q: A company runs a development environment that operates Monday through Friday, 9 AM to 6 PM. Instances are idle on weekends and evenings. How to reduce costs by at least 60%?
o: Use AWS Instance Scheduler to automatically stop instances outside business hours and start them when developers arrive, eliminating 70%+ of running hours
Keep instances running 24/7 on On-Demand pricing but switch to smaller instance types that are cheaper per hour
Purchase 3-year Reserved Instances for the development environment committing to pay for hours that are never used on weekends
Migrate all development workloads to Spot Instances which can be interrupted mid-development causing developers to lose unsaved work
e: Correct (A): Instance Scheduler: stop evenings + weekends = ~128 hours/week stopped out of 168 total. Only pay for ~40 hours (76% reduction). Exceeds 60% target. Zero wasted spend. B is wrong: Smaller instances running 24/7 still pay for 128 hours of idle time (evenings + weekends). Saves some cost but far less than 60% reduction. C is wrong: Reserved Instances commit to paying for all 8,760 hours/year. Dev environment only uses ~2,080 hours (40/week × 52). You'd pay for 6,680 hours of unused capacity. D is wrong: Spot Instances can be terminated with 2-minute notice during active development. Developers lose context-switching work. Not suitable for interactive development environments.

q: A solutions architect needs to store 100 TB of log data that is accessed frequently for the first 30 days, rarely accessed from day 31-90, and never accessed after 90 days but must be retained for 7 years. Which strategy minimizes storage cost?
o: S3 Lifecycle policy that keeps objects in S3 Standard for 30 days, transitions to S3 Standard-IA at day 30, transitions to S3 Glacier Deep Archive at day 90 for 7-year retention
Store all 100 TB in S3 Standard permanently for the full 7 years since the per-GB cost difference between tiers is negligible at this scale
Store everything in S3 Glacier Deep Archive from day one since it's the cheapest tier regardless of access pattern requirements
Delete all data after 90 days and recreate it from source systems if ever needed since storage costs exceed recreation costs
e: Correct (A): Lifecycle policy automates tiering: Standard ($0.023/GB) for active access → Standard-IA ($0.0125/GB) for rare access → Glacier Deep Archive ($0.00099/GB) for archive. Massive savings over 7 years. B is wrong: S3 Standard for 7 years at 100 TB = ~$2.3M. Glacier Deep Archive for the same = ~$99K. The difference is 23x - absolutely not negligible at 100 TB scale. C is wrong: Glacier Deep Archive has 12-hour retrieval time. First 30 days require frequent access (impossible with 12-hour waits). Access pattern doesn't match the tier. D is wrong: 7-year retention requirement means data cannot be deleted at 90 days. Compliance/legal requirements mandate the full retention period regardless of access frequency.

q: A company has multiple EC2 instances across various instance families (m5, c5, r5) with predictable baseline usage. They want maximum flexibility in their commitment discount. Which purchasing option?
o: Compute Savings Plans which commit to a dollar-per-hour spend and apply discounts automatically across any instance family, size, OS, Region, or tenancy
EC2 Instance Savings Plans which commit to a specific instance family in a specific Region providing less flexibility across families
Standard Reserved Instances which commit to a specific instance type and size in a specific Region with no flexibility to change families
Spot Instances which provide deep discounts but can be interrupted at any time and require fault-tolerant architecture
e: Correct (A): Compute Savings Plans: commit $/hr, applies to ANY instance family (m5, c5, r5), any size, any Region, any OS, even Fargate/Lambda. Maximum flexibility across diverse usage patterns. B is wrong: EC2 Instance Savings Plans lock to one instance family (e.g., m5 in us-east-1). This company uses m5, c5, AND r5 - they'd need three separate plans, reducing optimization. C is wrong: Standard RIs commit to exact instance type (e.g., m5.xlarge in us-east-1). Cannot apply to c5 or r5 usage. Least flexible option for mixed environments. D is wrong: Spot provides discounts but with interruption risk. Not suitable for predictable baseline workloads that need guaranteed availability.

q: A company uses NAT Gateways in each of their 6 Availability Zones for private subnet internet access. Monthly NAT Gateway costs are $500+. How to reduce this while maintaining availability?
o: Consolidate to fewer NAT Gateways (one per AZ is redundant if only 2-3 AZs have active workloads) and use VPC Gateway Endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB traffic to avoid NAT charges entirely
Increase the NAT Gateway size to process data faster and reduce hourly charges through higher throughput
Replace NAT Gateways with Internet Gateways on private subnets which would make them public and expose instances to the internet
Disable all NAT Gateways and block all outbound internet access from private subnets breaking patch downloads and API calls
e: Correct (A): Gateway Endpoints for S3/DynamoDB are free and bypass NAT entirely. If most traffic is S3/DynamoDB, NAT data processing charges drop dramatically. Consolidate unused AZ NATs. B is wrong: NAT Gateway doesn't have 'sizes' - it scales automatically. You pay per hour + per GB processed. Faster processing doesn't reduce GB volume charges. C is wrong: Attaching IGW routes to private subnets makes them public by definition. Instances become internet-reachable, violating the reason they're in private subnets. D is wrong: Disabling NAT breaks all outbound connectivity (OS patches, API calls, license validation, third-party integrations). Not a viable cost reduction.

q: A solutions architect discovers that a production RDS instance is using less than 10% CPU on average. The instance type is db.r5.4xlarge. What should they recommend?
o: Right-size the instance to a smaller type like db.r5.large or db.r5.xlarge using AWS Compute Optimizer recommendations, reducing costs by 75-85% while maintaining performance headroom
Increase the instance size to db.r5.8xlarge to ensure future growth capacity since the current instance might eventually need more CPU
Migrate from RDS to DynamoDB since low CPU usage means the relational database is not being used effectively
Keep the current instance size because right-sizing production databases is too risky regardless of the cost savings
e: Correct (A): 10% average CPU on db.r5.4xlarge (16 vCPU, 128 GB RAM) = massively over-provisioned. Right-size to r5.large (2 vCPU, 16 GB) or r5.xlarge (4 vCPU, 32 GB). Monitor after change. 75-85% savings. B is wrong: Increasing size when utilization is 10% wastes even more money. Provision for actual demand plus reasonable headroom (30-40%), not 10x+ over-provisioning. C is wrong: Low CPU doesn't mean wrong database type. The workload might require relational features (joins, transactions, ACID). DynamoDB migration requires application rewrite. D is wrong: Right-sizing is standard practice. Change instance type during maintenance window with Multi-AZ failover. Minutes of impact. 75-85% savings justify the minimal risk.

q: A company runs batch data processing jobs that take 2-4 hours each, are fault-tolerant, and can be restarted if interrupted. Jobs run daily. Which EC2 purchasing option saves the most money?
o: Spot Instances which provide up to 90% discount and are ideal because the workload is fault-tolerant, can be restarted on interruption, and doesn't require guaranteed continuous runtime
On-Demand Instances which provide guaranteed availability but at full price when the workload doesn't require guaranteed uninterrupted execution
Reserved Instances with 3-year commitment which requires paying for 24 hours daily when jobs only run 2-4 hours per day
Dedicated Hosts which provide physical server isolation at premium pricing far exceeding On-Demand for no additional benefit to batch processing
e: Correct (A): Fault-tolerant + restartable + daily batch = perfect Spot use case. Up to 90% off On-Demand. If interrupted, job restarts from checkpoint. Cost reduction massive for 2-4 hour daily jobs. B is wrong: On-Demand works but pays full price. The workload tolerates interruption (Spot's downside), so paying for guaranteed availability provides no value. Pure waste. C is wrong: 3-year RI pays for 24 hours/day when you use 2-4 hours. Paying for 20 idle hours daily. Far more expensive than Spot for limited-hour workloads. D is wrong: Dedicated Hosts provide hardware isolation for licensing compliance. No benefit for batch processing. Costs more than On-Demand, opposite of cost optimization.

q: A company has a web application with unpredictable traffic - sometimes 1,000 requests/second, sometimes 10 requests/second. They want to minimize cost without over-provisioning. Which compute approach?
o: AWS Lambda for the API layer which scales automatically to match traffic and charges only for actual invocations with zero cost during idle periods
A fleet of 20 EC2 On-Demand instances running 24/7 sized for peak 1,000 RPS that sit mostly idle at 10 RPS
EC2 Reserved Instances sized for peak capacity which locks in the same over-provisioned fleet at a discounted but still wasteful rate
A single EC2 instance that handles 10 RPS normally but crashes under 1,000 RPS peak load causing outages
e: Correct (A): Lambda: scales from 10 to 1,000+ concurrent executions instantly. Pay per invocation (no idle cost). At 10 RPS you pay for 10 invocations/sec. At 0 RPS you pay nothing. Perfect for unpredictable traffic. B is wrong: 20 instances for peak means 19 instances sit idle most of the time (10 RPS ÷ ~50 RPS per instance = 1 instance needed). 95% waste during low traffic. C is wrong: Reserved Instances for 20 instances = 3-year commitment to pay for over-provisioned fleet. Saves vs On-Demand but still paying for massive idle capacity. D is wrong: Single instance saves money at low traffic but fails at peak. Outages during high traffic make this non-viable regardless of cost savings.

q: A solutions architect needs to reduce data transfer costs between two VPCs in the same Region that communicate heavily. Currently using VPC peering. What additional optimization reduces costs?
o: Ensure resources communicate using private IP addresses over the peering connection since data transfer within the same AZ using private IPs is free
Route all inter-VPC traffic through the public internet via Internet Gateways which charges higher data transfer rates than VPC peering
Add a NAT Gateway between the VPCs which adds per-GB data processing charges on top of existing transfer costs
Replicate all data from VPC-A into VPC-B eliminating the need for cross-VPC communication but doubling storage costs
e: Correct (A): Same-AZ private IP traffic = free. Cross-AZ = $0.01/GB each direction. Place communicating resources in same AZ. Private IPs over peering = lowest possible cost. B is wrong: Public internet traffic costs more than private ($0.09/GB outbound vs $0.01/GB cross-AZ). Going public increases cost and adds latency. C is wrong: NAT Gateway adds $0.045/GB processing charge on top of any transfer costs. It's for internet access, not VPC-to-VPC communication. D is wrong: Duplicating all data doubles storage costs and requires synchronization logic. Peering with AZ alignment is far simpler and cheaper.

q: A company stores 500 TB in S3 Standard but analysis shows 80% of objects haven't been accessed in over 60 days. They don't know future access patterns. Which S3 storage class should they move the cold data to?
o: S3 Intelligent-Tiering which automatically moves objects between frequent and infrequent access tiers based on actual access patterns with no retrieval fees
S3 Glacier Deep Archive which provides the lowest cost but has 12-hour retrieval time making it unsuitable if objects might be accessed unexpectedly
S3 One Zone-IA which is cheaper but stores data in only one AZ risking data loss if that AZ has a failure
S3 Standard-IA which saves money on storage but charges retrieval fees and requires you to know access patterns upfront which they don't
e: Correct (A): Intelligent-Tiering: monitors access patterns per object, moves to IA after 30 days idle, moves back to Frequent on access. No retrieval fees. Perfect when you don't know future patterns. Small monitoring fee. B is wrong: Glacier Deep Archive = 12-hour retrieval. If any of the 80% cold objects are accessed unexpectedly, users wait 12 hours. Unacceptable for unknown access patterns. C is wrong: One Zone-IA sacrifices durability (single AZ). At 500 TB, AZ failure risk means potential massive data loss. Not worth the small savings vs Standard-IA. D is wrong: Standard-IA charges per-GB retrieval fees. Unknown access patterns mean unpredictable costs. If objects become hot again, retrieval charges accumulate.

q: A company runs an ECS cluster with Fargate tasks for a web API. Average CPU utilization across tasks is 15%. How to reduce costs while maintaining scalability?
o: Reduce the vCPU and memory allocated to each Fargate task definition to match actual utilization, and configure auto-scaling to add tasks during demand spikes
Increase task CPU and memory allocations to run fewer total tasks hoping consolidation reduces cost even though each task is over-provisioned
Migrate from Fargate to EC2 launch type with Reserved Instances which adds management overhead for a workload with 15% utilization
Remove auto-scaling and run a fixed number of tasks to simplify billing even though it means over-provisioning during off-peak
e: Correct (A): Fargate charges per vCPU-hour and GB-hour. Right-size task definitions to actual usage (smaller vCPU/memory) = immediate cost reduction. Auto-scaling ensures capacity during peaks. B is wrong: Larger tasks cost more per-unit and don't reduce total cost. 15% utilization means you're already over-provisioned - increasing allocation makes it worse. C is wrong: EC2 launch type with 15% utilization means Reserved Instances pay for 85% idle capacity. Also adds patching, scaling, and cluster management overhead. D is wrong: Fixed task count without scaling means provisioning for peak 24/7. At 15% average utilization, you're paying for capacity unused 85% of the time.

q: A company has a data analytics workload that queries 2 TB of data in S3 daily using Athena. Most queries only need 3 columns out of 50. How to reduce Athena costs by over 90%?
o: Convert data from CSV to Apache Parquet columnar format so Athena reads only the 3 required columns (6% of data) instead of all 50 columns
Increase Athena query concurrency limits to process queries faster which doesn't reduce the per-query cost based on data scanned
Move all data from S3 to Amazon RDS and query there which costs more in compute and storage than Athena for 2 TB daily analytics
Compress CSV files with gzip which reduces file size but Athena still reads all 50 columns unlike columnar format which reads only needed columns
e: Correct (A): Parquet is columnar: reading 3 of 50 columns scans only 6% of data. Athena charges $5 per TB scanned. 2 TB CSV = $10/day. Parquet (3/50 columns) = ~$0.60/day. 94% reduction. B is wrong: Concurrency controls parallelism, not cost. Each query still scans 2 TB regardless of how fast it runs. Cost = data scanned, not execution time. C is wrong: Loading 2 TB into RDS requires large instance ($$$), plus RDS isn't designed for analytical workloads (no columnar format, no parallel scan).
